content:
# Numbers Chapter 23 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 176-177 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_023_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0130-0131 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### Preparations for the First Oracle (vv. 1-6)

**23:1** And Balaam said to Balak, "Build for me here seven altars, and prepare for me here seven bulls and seven rams."

**23:2** And Balak did as Balaam had spoken. And Balak and Balaam offered up a bull and a ram on each altar.

**23:3** And Balaam said to Balak, "Station yourself by your burnt offering, and I will go. Perhaps YHWH will come to meet me, and whatever he shows me I will tell you." And he went to a bare height.

**23:4** And God met Balaam, and he said to him, "The seven altars I have arranged, and I have offered up a bull and a ram on each altar."

**23:5** And YHWH put a word in the mouth of Balaam and said, "Return to Balak, and thus you shall speak."

**23:6** And he returned to him, and behold, he was standing by his burnt offering, he and all the princes of Moab.

---

### Balaam's First Oracle (vv. 7-10)

**23:7** And he took up his oracle and said:

"From Aram, Balak has brought me,
the king of Moab from the mountains of the east:
'Come, curse Jacob for me;
and come, denounce Israel.'

**23:8** How shall I curse whom God has not cursed?
And how shall I denounce whom YHWH has not denounced?

**23:9** For from the top of the rocks I see him,
and from the hills I behold him.
Behold, a people that dwells alone,
and among the nations does not reckon itself.

**23:10** Who has counted the dust of Jacob,
or numbered the fourth part of Israel?
Let my soul die the death of the upright,
and let my end be like his!"

---

### Balak's First Protest (vv. 11-12)

**23:11** And Balak said to Balaam, "What have you done to me? To curse my enemies I took you, and behold, blessing you have blessed!"

**23:12** And he answered and said, "Is it not that which YHWH puts in my mouth -- that I must take heed to speak?"

---

### Relocation and Preparations for the Second Oracle (vv. 13-17)

**23:13** And Balak said to him, "Come, please, with me to another place from which you will see them. Only the end of them you shall see, but the whole of them you shall not see. And curse them for me from there."

**23:14** And he took him to the field of Zophim, to the top of Pisgah. And he built seven altars and offered up a bull and a ram on each altar.

**23:15** And he said to Balak, "Station yourself here by your burnt offering, and I will seek encounter over there."

**23:16** And YHWH met Balaam and put a word in his mouth and said, "Return to Balak, and thus you shall speak."

**23:17** And he came to him, and behold, he was standing by his burnt offering, and the princes of Moab with him. And Balak said to him, "What has YHWH spoken?"

---

### Balaam's Second Oracle (vv. 18-24)

**23:18** And he took up his oracle and said:

"Rise, Balak, and hear;
give ear to me, son of Zippor.

**23:19** God is not a man that he should lie,
nor a son of man that he should repent.
Has he said, and will he not do it?
And has he spoken, and will he not establish it?

**23:20** Behold, to bless I have received command;
and he has blessed, and I cannot reverse it.

**23:21** He has not beheld iniquity in Jacob,
and he has not seen trouble in Israel.
YHWH his God is with him,
and the shout of a king is among them.

**23:22** God who brings them out from Egypt --
he has strength like the horns of the wild ox.

**23:23** For there is no enchantment against Jacob,
and no divination against Israel.
At the appointed time it shall be said of Jacob
and of Israel, 'What has God wrought!'

**23:24** Behold, a people -- as a lioness it rises,
and as a lion it lifts itself up.
It does not lie down until it devours the prey
and drinks the blood of the slain."

---

### Balak's Second Protest (vv. 25-26)

**23:25** And Balak said to Balaam, "Neither cursing shall you curse them, nor blessing shall you bless them!"

**23:26** And Balaam answered and said to Balak, "Did I not speak to you, saying, 'All that YHWH speaks, that I must do'?"

---

### Preparations for the Third Attempt (vv. 27-30)

**23:27** And Balak said to Balaam, "Come, please, I will take you to another place. Perhaps it will be right in the eyes of God that you may curse them for me from there."

**23:28** And Balak took Balaam to the top of Peor, which overlooks the face of the Jeshimon.

**23:29** And Balaam said to Balak, "Build for me here seven altars, and prepare for me here seven bulls and seven rams."

**23:30** And Balak did as Balaam had said, and he offered up a bull and a ram on each altar.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Overall Assessment

All three translators -- EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA -- worked faithfully from the Leningrad Codex (pages 176-177) and cross-referenced the Codex Vaticanus (pa_0130-0131). The manuscript images confirm the standard Masoretic text with the three-column layout, full Tiberian vocalization, and no unusual scribal features or corrections visible in this chapter. The Vaticanus pages show the Greek text in uncial scriptio continua with section markers visible (including what appears to be section numbering in the margins at pa_0131 corresponding to the opening of Balaam's oracles). Agreement among the three translators is strong across all thirty verses; the narrative prose sections (vv. 1-6, 11-17, 25-30) produced near-identical renderings, while the poetic oracles (vv. 7-10, 18-24) showed the most meaningful variation.

### Key Decisions

1. **Verse 3 -- "bare height" vs. "barren hilltop" (Hebrew: shephi):** EMET renders "barren height," LOGOS "bare height," PNEUMA "barren hilltop." The Hebrew shephi denotes a stripped, windswept elevation. The consensus adopts "bare height" as the most faithful and concise rendering -- "barren hilltop" adds interpretive content ("hilltop") not explicitly in the Hebrew, and "barren height" is acceptable but "bare" is slightly closer to the sense of stripped/exposed terrain. The LXX's divergent rendering (eutheian, "straight/directly") was noted but not followed, as the MT is clear.

2. **Verse 5 -- "in the mouth of Balaam" vs. "in Balaam's mouth":** EMET preserves the Hebrew prepositional phrase "in the mouth of Balaam" (be-fi Bil'am); LOGOS and PNEUMA render "in the mouth of Balaam" and "in Balaam's mouth" respectively. The consensus follows EMET's more literal form "in the mouth of Balaam" to preserve the Hebrew word order, which emphasizes the mouth as the receptacle of divine speech (cf. Jer 1:9).

3. **Verse 6 -- "princes" vs. "officials" (Hebrew: sarei):** EMET and LOGOS render sarei Mo'av as "princes of Moab"; PNEUMA renders "officials of Moab." The consensus adopts "princes" as the standard translation of sarim in the context of a royal court, consistent with the rendering throughout the Balaam narrative.

4. **Verse 15 -- Rendering of the niphal iqqareh:** EMET renders "I shall encounter," LOGOS "I will meet," PNEUMA "I go to meet YHWH." The consensus renders "I will seek encounter" to capture the niphal sense (allowing oneself to be met) while remaining intelligible.

5. **Verse 19 -- "repent" vs. "change his mind" (Hebrew: yitnekham):** See Disagreement Log. The consensus adopts "repent" as the formal equivalent of the hitpael of nakham, preserving the theological weight of the term and its canonical resonance (cf. 1 Sam 15:29).

6. **Verse 21 -- "his God" vs. "their God" (Hebrew: Elohav):** EMET and LOGOS render "YHWH his God is with him" (singular, referring to Israel as a collective); PNEUMA renders "YHWH their God is with them" (plural, distributive). The Hebrew uses singular suffixes (Elohav, immo), treating Israel as a corporate singular. The consensus preserves the singular "his God... with him" to maintain fidelity to the Hebrew morphology, while noting in the Disagreement Log that the plural reading is defensible.

7. **Verse 22 -- Participial vs. past tense for motsi'am:** EMET renders "bringing them out" (participial), LOGOS "who brought them out" (past), PNEUMA "who brought them out" (past). The Hebrew motsi'am is a hiphil participle, suggesting an ongoing or characterizing action. The consensus renders "who brings them out" to preserve the participial aspect -- the exodus is presented as a defining, continuing act of God's character, not merely a past event.

8. **Verse 22 -- Rendering of ke-to'afot re'em:** EMET "as the horns of the wild ox are to him," LOGOS "like the horns of the wild ox," PNEUMA "strength like the wild ox." The consensus adopts "he has strength like the horns of the wild ox" to preserve both the rare noun to'afot (horns/lofty projections) and the animal re'em (wild ox/aurochs), while making the metaphor's application clear.

9. **Verse 23 -- "enchantment" vs. "sorcery" vs. "divination" (Hebrew: nakhash and qesem):** See Disagreement Log. The consensus distinguishes the two terms: nakhash as "enchantment" (omen-reading) and qesem as "divination" (lot-casting/sorcery), following LOGOS. The preposition be- is rendered "against" rather than "in" to capture the contextual sense that these hostile arts cannot prevail against Israel.

10. **Verse 25 -- Infinitive absolute constructions:** All three translators handle the double infinitive absolute differently. EMET preserves the Hebrew doubling structure directly. LOGOS uses "at all" for emphasis. PNEUMA restructures as conditional. The consensus renders "Neither cursing shall you curse them, nor blessing shall you bless them!" preserving the infinitive absolute doubling per binding conventions.

### Manuscript Notes

The Leningrad Codex pages 176-177 confirm the standard MT reading throughout. Numbers 23 begins on page 176 continuing from the end of chapter 22, extending through page 177. The three-column layout is maintained with Masorah parva in the intercolumnar margins. A setumah division is visible after verse 12 (end of first oracle cycle) and after verse 26 (end of second oracle cycle), confirming the literary structure. The poetic oracle sections (vv. 7-10, 18-24) are written in continuous prose format, not stichometric layout. No ketiv/qere notations or scribal corrections are visible for this chapter.

The Codex Vaticanus (pa_0130-0131) follows the MT closely in narrative structure. The most significant LXX divergences noted across all three translators include: (1) sperma ("seed") for afar ("dust") at v. 10; (2) demous ("peoples") for rova ("fourth part") at v. 10; (3) monokeros ("unicorn/one-horned") for re'em ("wild ox") at v. 22; (4) the possible reading martys ("witness") for adai ("unto me") at v. 18; and (5) the rendering of aven/amal as "toil/pain" rather than "iniquity/trouble" at v. 21. In each case the consensus follows the MT as the primary text.

---

## Disagreement Log

### Verse 3 -- "bare height" vs. "barren hilltop" (shephi)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a barren height" |
| LOGOS | "a bare height" |
| PNEUMA | "a barren hilltop" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "a bare height." The Hebrew shephi means a stripped, exposed elevation. "Bare height" (LOGOS) is the most concise and faithful rendering. PNEUMA's "barren hilltop" adds the interpretive specification "hilltop" not present in the Hebrew. EMET's "barren height" is acceptable but "bare" better captures the sense of stripped/exposed rather than "barren" which can imply infertility. The LXX reads eutheian ("straight/directly"), a divergent interpretation not followed here.

### Verse 7 -- "oracle" vs. "discourse" vs. "prophetic utterance" (mashal)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "his oracle" |
| LOGOS | "his discourse" |
| PNEUMA | "his prophetic utterance" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "his oracle." The Hebrew mashal in the Balaam cycle specifically denotes a formal prophetic oracle under divine compulsion -- not a general "discourse" (too broad) or "prophetic utterance" (too interpretive and lengthy). "Oracle" captures both the formal and mantic character of the mashal. This term is used consistently at vv. 7, 18, and will recur at 24:3, 15, 20, 21, 23.

### Verse 7 -- "leads me" vs. "has brought me" (yankheni)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "leads me" |
| LOGOS | "has brought me" |
| PNEUMA | "has brought me" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "has brought me." The Hebrew yankheni (from nakhah, "to lead/guide") is in the imperfect used with past narrative sense in poetry. "Has brought me" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) better fits the context -- Balaam has already arrived -- while "leads me" (EMET) could imply the journey is ongoing. The LXX metepempsato ("summoned") also supports a completed action.

### Verse 9 -- "Behold" vs. "Look" (hen)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold" |
| LOGOS | "Behold" |
| PNEUMA | "Look" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Behold." The Hebrew presentative particle hen is a formal, elevated term appropriate to prophetic poetry. "Behold" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the register of oracular speech, while "Look" (PNEUMA) is too colloquial for this context. "Behold" is used consistently throughout the oracle sections.

### Verse 9 -- "dwelling alone" vs. "dwells apart" (levadad yishkon)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "that alone shall dwell" |
| LOGOS | "dwelling alone" |
| PNEUMA | "that dwells apart" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "that dwells alone." This combines the natural English word order with the key term "alone" (levadad) rather than "apart" (PNEUMA), which slightly alters the semantic field. The Hebrew levadad means "alone, solitary, in isolation" -- it is the word used of God in Deuteronomy 32:12. "Apart" introduces a relational nuance ("separated from") that goes slightly beyond the Hebrew.

### Verse 10 -- "Who has counted" vs. "Who can count" (mi manah)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Who has counted" (perfect) |
| LOGOS | "Who can count" (modal) |
| PNEUMA | "Who can count" (modal) |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Who has counted." The Hebrew mi manah uses the perfect tense (completed action), not a modal. The rhetorical question asks who has accomplished the counting -- implying no one has, because it is impossible. The perfect tense (EMET) is more faithful to the Hebrew morphology than the English modal "can count" (LOGOS/PNEUMA), which shifts from accomplished fact to theoretical ability.

### Verse 10 -- "or numbered" vs. "and number" (u-mispar)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and number" |
| LOGOS | "or number" |
| PNEUMA | "or number even" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "or numbered." The Hebrew conjunction vav here functions as "or" in the parallel rhetorical question (following LOGOS), and the verb is best rendered in the past tense to match "who has counted" in the first colon. PNEUMA's addition of "even" is interpretive but captures the intensification of the question. The consensus omits "even" as an addition not present in the Hebrew.

### Verse 10 -- "like his" vs. "like theirs" (kamohu)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "like his" (singular) |
| LOGOS | "like his" (singular) |
| PNEUMA | "like theirs" (plural) |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "like his." The Hebrew kamohu is a third-person masculine singular suffix, referring to Israel as a corporate singular entity. PNEUMA's "like theirs" makes the referent distributively plural, which is a defensible English rendering but departs from the Hebrew morphology. The singular is preserved for consistency with the corporate-singular treatment of Israel throughout the oracles.

### Verse 11 -- Infinitive absolute rendering (barekh berakhta)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "blessing you have blessed" |
| LOGOS | "you have surely blessed them" |
| PNEUMA | "you have done nothing but bless them, yes, bless them" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "blessing you have blessed." Per binding conventions, the infinitive absolute is rendered with doubling. EMET's rendering directly mirrors the Hebrew construction. LOGOS's "surely blessed" captures the emphasis but not the form. PNEUMA's paraphrase is too expansive for a consensus translation.

### Verse 13 -- "them" vs. "him" (Hebrew suffixes alternate between singular and plural)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "him" (singular throughout) |
| LOGOS | "them" (plural throughout) |
| PNEUMA | "them" (plural throughout) |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "them." Although the Hebrew uses singular suffixes in several places (treating Israel as a collective), the context of Balak speaking about "my enemies" (plural, v. 11) and the English reader's expectation make "them" the clearer rendering. The singular-collective usage is a Hebrew idiom that would create confusion in English. LOGOS and PNEUMA are followed here.

### Verse 15 -- Rendering of iqqareh koh

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "I shall encounter there" |
| LOGOS | "I will meet yonder" |
| PNEUMA | "I go to meet YHWH over there" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "I will seek encounter over there." The niphal of qarah (iqqareh) means "to allow oneself to be met" or "to seek encounter" -- Balaam positions himself to be found by God. PNEUMA's explicit addition of "YHWH" is not in the Hebrew. LOGOS's "yonder" is archaic. The consensus captures the niphal sense with "seek encounter" while using the natural "over there" for koh.

### Verse 17 -- "What has YHWH spoken?" vs. "What did YHWH say?"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "What did YHWH speak?" |
| LOGOS | "What has YHWH spoken?" |
| PNEUMA | "What did YHWH say?" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "What has YHWH spoken?" The Hebrew mah dibber YHWH uses the piel of davar ("to speak"), not 'amar ("to say"). "Spoken" (LOGOS) is more precise than "say" (PNEUMA), and the English perfect tense "has spoken" better conveys the completed divine act whose content Balak now inquires about.

### Verse 18 -- "give ear to me" vs. "give me your ear" (ha'azinah adai)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "give ear unto me" |
| LOGOS | "give ear to me" |
| PNEUMA | "give me your ear" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "give ear to me." The Hebrew ha'azinah adai is a solemn imperative. "Give ear to me" (LOGOS) preserves the formal register of prophetic address without the archaism of "unto" (EMET) or the colloquial inversion of "give me your ear" (PNEUMA).

### Verse 19 -- "repent" vs. "change his mind" (yitnekham)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "repent" |
| LOGOS | "repent" |
| PNEUMA | "change his mind" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "repent." The hitpael of nakham (yitnekham) means "to feel regret, to relent, to change one's mind." Both "repent" and "change his mind" are defensible. The consensus adopts "repent" (EMET/LOGOS) because: (a) it is the traditional rendering with deep canonical resonance (cf. 1 Sam 15:29, "the Glory of Israel does not lie or repent"); (b) "change his mind" (PNEUMA), while clearer in modern English, loses the theological weight and the connection to the broader biblical vocabulary of divine constancy; (c) the parallel with "lie" (yekhazzev) works better with the more compact "repent" than the phrasal "change his mind."

### Verse 19 -- "establish it" vs. "make it good" vs. "make it stand" (yeqimenah)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "make it stand" |
| LOGOS | "establish it" |
| PNEUMA | "make it good" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "establish it." The hiphil of qum (yeqimenah) means "to cause to stand, to establish, to fulfill." "Establish" (LOGOS) is the most precise English equivalent -- it captures both the "standing" metaphor and the sense of fulfillment. "Make it stand" (EMET) is more literal but awkward in English. "Make it good" (PNEUMA) is a dynamic rendering that loses the root metaphor.

### Verse 20 -- "to bless I have received command" vs. "to bless I have received" vs. "I received a command to bless"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to bless I have received" |
| LOGOS | "to bless I have received command" |
| PNEUMA | "I received a command to bless" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "to bless I have received command." The Hebrew barekh laqakhti literally means "to bless I have taken/received." LOGOS's addition of "command" makes explicit what is implied by the context -- Balaam has received a divine commission. The fronted infinitive ("to bless I have received") preserves the emphatic Hebrew word order, which PNEUMA's restructuring loses. EMET's bare "I have received" leaves the object unstated in a way that may confuse English readers.

### Verse 21 -- "his God... with him" vs. "their God... with them" (Elohav... immo)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "his God... with him" (singular) |
| LOGOS | "his God... with him" (singular) |
| PNEUMA | "their God... with them" (plural) |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "his God... with him." The Hebrew suffixes are unambiguously third-person masculine singular. Israel is treated as a corporate entity. PNEUMA's plural is a valid dynamic rendering for English clarity, but the consensus preserves the Hebrew morphology, as the corporate-singular treatment of Israel is theologically significant in the oracle context.

### Verse 21 -- "among them" vs. "in him" (bo)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in him" |
| LOGOS | "among them" |
| PNEUMA | "among them" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "among them." While the Hebrew bo is singular ("in him"), the phrase "the shout of a king is in him" is opaque in English. "Among them" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) clarifies that the royal shout resounds within the people of Israel without altering the meaning. This is a concession to English idiom while the preceding line preserves the singular.

### Verse 22 -- Participial rendering of motsi'am

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "is bringing them out" (present progressive) |
| LOGOS | "who brought them out" (past) |
| PNEUMA | "who brought them out" (past) |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "who brings them out." The Hebrew motsi'am is a hiphil participle, presenting the exodus as a characterizing act of God -- not merely a completed past event. The consensus uses the English simple present "brings" to reflect the participial aspect, following the insight of EMET while using a more natural English construction than "is bringing."

### Verse 22 -- Rendering of ke-to'afot re'em lo

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "as the horns of the wild ox are to him" |
| LOGOS | "is for them like the horns of the wild ox" |
| PNEUMA | "he has strength like the wild ox" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "he has strength like the horns of the wild ox." The Hebrew ke-to'afot re'em lo is compressed and difficult. PNEUMA's rendering captures the sense but drops "horns" (to'afot). EMET's rendering is literal but syntactically awkward. The consensus combines the best elements: "strength" makes the metaphor's application explicit, "horns of the wild ox" preserves the rare noun to'afot and the specific animal re'em. The subject "he" refers to God.

### Verse 23 -- "enchantment/divination" vs. "sorcery/divination" vs. "sorcery that works against"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "divination... sorcery" (nakhash, qesem) |
| LOGOS | "enchantment... divination" |
| PNEUMA | "sorcery that works against... divination that prevails against" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "enchantment... divination." EMET reverses the standard semantic mapping (nakhash is typically omen-reading/enchantment; qesem is divination/lot-casting). LOGOS correctly distinguishes the two terms. PNEUMA adds interpretive verbs ("works against," "prevails against") not in the Hebrew. The consensus follows LOGOS's precise distinction. The preposition be- is rendered "against" to capture the hostile context.

### Verse 23 -- "What has God wrought!" vs. "What has God done!" vs. "Look what God has done!"

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "What has God done!" |
| LOGOS | "What has God wrought!" |
| PNEUMA | "Look what God has done!" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "What has God wrought!" The Hebrew mah pa'al El uses the verb pa'al, which means "to work, to accomplish, to make." The English "wrought" (past participle of "work") is the most precise equivalent and preserves the register of elevated speech appropriate to prophetic oracle. "Done" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adequate but generic. PNEUMA's prefixed "Look" is not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 24 -- "lioness" vs. "lion cub" (lavi)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a lioness" |
| LOGOS | "a lioness" |
| PNEUMA | "a lioness" (in notes: "lion in its prime") |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "a lioness." All three agree on the primary rendering. The Hebrew lavi is traditionally understood as the lioness (the hunter of the pride). The LXX renders skulmnos ("lion's cub"), but the MT reading as "lioness" is followed.

### Verse 25 -- Rendering of double infinitive absolute (gam qov lo tiqqovenu gam barekh lo tevarakhenu)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Neither cursing you shall curse him, nor blessing you shall bless him" |
| LOGOS | "Neither curse them at all, nor bless them at all" |
| PNEUMA | "If you will not curse them at all, then at least do not bless them at all" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Neither cursing shall you curse them, nor blessing shall you bless them!" Per binding conventions, the infinitive absolute doubling is preserved. EMET comes closest to the Hebrew structure. LOGOS's "at all" captures the emphasis differently. PNEUMA restructures as a conditional, departing from the Hebrew syntax. The consensus preserves the doubled form while rendering the suffixes as "them" (plural) for English clarity.

### Verse 27 -- "that you will curse" vs. "that you may curse" (u-qavotho)

| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall curse him" |
| LOGOS | "you will curse them" |
| PNEUMA | "for you to curse them" |

**SOPHIA's decision:** "that you may curse them." The Hebrew is introduced by ulai ("perhaps"), establishing contingency. The subjunctive "may curse" best captures Balak's uncertain hope. EMET's "shall" and LOGOS's "will" are too definite for a clause governed by "perhaps."

---

*-- SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter*
